\xf0\x9f\x94\xa5Bankruptcy Bombs Dropping!!\xf0\x9f\x94\xa5
3 min

\xf0\x9f\x94\xa5Bankruptcy Bombs Dropping!!\xf0\x9f\x94\xa5

Forwarded this email? Subscribe here for more

Isn\xe2\x80\x99t this paywall annoying? Join thousands of lawyers, investment bankers, financial advisors, investors and others who have access to all of our content:

For group rates, email us at petition@petition11.com.

Robertshaw + Incora + Purdue Pharma & More.

Jun 30
READ IN APP

What a month for the bankruptcy industry.

As some of the bigger cases in recent memory roll off \xe2\x80\x94 congratulations, for instance, to everyone involved in achieving confirmed plans of reorganization in the Rite-Aid Corporation and Cano Health chapter 11 bankruptcy cases \xe2\x80\x94 and the industry continues to deal with higher interest rates for longer (mostly in the form of low-to-middle market sale cases in-court or, alternatively, liability management exercises out of court), some recent court decisions have the potential to upend bankruptcy practice as we know it.

On June 20, 2024, Judge Lopez in Texas issued a long-awaited memorandum decision and order in the adversary proceeding between Invesco Senior Secured Management Inc. (\xe2\x80\x9cInvesco\xe2\x80\x9d) on one hand, and Robertshaw US Holding Corp. (\xe2\x80\x9cRobertshaw\xe2\x80\x9d), One Rock Capital Partners (\xe2\x80\x9cOne Rock\xe2\x80\x9d) and certain other \xe2\x80\x9clender plaintiffs\xe2\x80\x9d (Bain Capital LP, Eaton Vance Management, and Canyon Partners LLC) on the other hand. We covered this sh*t show of a double uptier liability management fiasco in depth here\xe2\x80\xa6

\xf0\x9f\x92\xa5Another Failed Liability Management Exercise (RobertShaw).\xf0\x9f\x92\xa5

PETITION LLC
\xc2\xb7
Feb 21
Read full story

\xe2\x80\xa6 it\xe2\x80\x99s worth revisiting \xe2\x80\xa6 and subsequently just sat back and watched the gloves come off during the course of months of costly litigation.

High level, here\xe2\x80\x99s what you need to know:

\xf0\x9f\x93\x8dIn May \xe2\x80\x9823, Robertshaw, Invesco, the lender plaintiffs and One Rock engaged in the first of two uptier transactions, providing first out new-money loans and exchanging existing first and second lien loans into new super-priority second and third out loans.
\xf0\x9f\x93\x8dInvesco subsequently acquired a majority in principal amount of the outstanding first and second out loans so that it would have \xe2\x80\x9crequired lender\xe2\x80\x9d status. It later became clear from arguments that Invesco didn\xe2\x80\x99t have a whole lot of faith in Robertshaw\xe2\x80\x99s go-forward prospects and ability to avoid bankruptcy, so it was strategically positioning itself to be in pole position to backstop a DIP (and derive all of the sweet SWEET economic benefits therefrom) and eventually own the business (via credit bid, with breakup protections should someone come in higher or better).
\xf0\x9f\x93\x8dBut Invesco wasn\xe2\x80\x99t slick.
\xf0\x9f\x93\x8dThe lender plaintiffs found out about Invesco\xe2\x80\x99s machinations and quickly went on the offensive; they approached Robertshaw and embarked on a series of deals that effectively stripped Invesco of \xe2\x80\x9crequired lender\xe2\x80\x9d status and shifted it back over to them \xe2\x80\x94 and by \xe2\x80\x9cstripped,\xe2\x80\x9d which sounds highly negative, we mean paid off Invesco\xe2\x80\x99s first out loans with a premium, LOL. In many respects you have to admire the f*ckery of everyone here: first the company formed a new entity owned by Robertshaw (without voting interest, that went to a parent entity \xe2\x80\x94 does this make it a \xe2\x80\x9csubsidiary?,\xe2\x80\x9d \xf0\x9f\xa4\x94); second, the lender plaintiffs loaned that new entity $228mm; third, the new entity dividended the $228mm to Robertshaw which used the proceeds to prepay the super-priority first-out loans in an amount that eliminated Invesco\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9crequired lender\xe2\x80\x9d status. Now the \xe2\x80\x9crequired lenders,\xe2\x80\x9d the lender plaintiffs amended the credit agreement to allow Robertshaw to incur $228mm of new debt and repay the initial $228mm loan to the new entity. This structure was a workaround designed to avoid a covenant breach in the credit agreement which prohibited Robertshaw and its \xe2\x80\x9csubsidiaries\xe2\x80\x9d from incurring new debt.
\xf0\x9f\x93\x8dInvesco was like, \xe2\x80\x9cwhat the holy f*ck\xe2\x80\x9d and sued the lender plaintiffs and One Rock in New York. Nevermind it got paid out at par plus on the first lien loans, the deal implicated other potential economics!
\xf0\x9f\x93\x8dTwo months later Robertshaw filed for bankruptcy, making this yet another embarrassing example of a company that embarked on a comprehensive LME transaction (two actually) only to swirl around the dirtiest downtrodden bankruptcy bin imaginable. We\xe2\x80\x99re talking like straight out of Trainspotting, folks. The Robertshaw debtors \xe2\x80\x94 along with One Rock and the lender plaintiffs \xe2\x80\x94 filed an adversary proceeding against Invesco, to which Invesco shot over some counterclaims. Everyone wanted declaratory judgements! Everyone wanted to know what the bloody hell Judge Lopez was going to do in this high profile situation! Would he play ball with the debtor and the lender plaintiffs and establish his debtor-friendly bona fides?
\xf0\x9f\x93\x8dMost people in the market seemed to suspect that he would, though Judge Lopez took offense at the mere suggestion that he might be a sure shot booster for the debtors.
\xf0\x9f\x93\x8dJudge Lopez did conclude that the newly formed entity was a \xe2\x80\x9csubsidiary\xe2\x80\x9d for purposes of the credit agreement and therefore found that the loan to the newly formed entity breached the credit agreement. Invesco be like:

\xf0\x9f\x93\x8dJudge Lopez be like\xe2\x80\xa6

nd One Rock engaged in the first of two uptier transactions, providing first out new- money loans and exchanging existing first and second lien loans into new super- priority second and third out loans. \xf0\x9f\x93\x8dInvesco subsequently acquired a majority in principal amount of the outstanding first and second out loans so that it would have \xe2\x80\x9crequired lender\xe2\x80\x9d status. It later became clear from arguments that Invesco didn\xe2\x80\x99t have a whole lot of faith in Robertshaw\xe2\x80\x99s go- forward prospects and ability to avoid bankruptcy, so it was strategically positioning itself to be in pole position to backstop a DIP (and derive all of the sweet SWEET economic benefits therefrom) and eventually own the business (via credit bid, with breakup protections should someone come in higher or better). \xf0\x9f\x93\x8dBut Invesco wasn\xe2\x80\x99t slick. \xf0\x9f\x93\x8dThe lender plaintiffs found out about Invesco\xe2\x80\x99s machinations and quickly went on the offensive; they approached Robertshaw and embarked on a series of deals that effectively stripped Invesco of \xe2\x80\x9crequired lender\xe2\x80\x9d status and shifted it back over to them and by \xe2\x80\x9cstripped,\xe2\x80\x9d which sounds highly negative, we mean paid off Invesco\xe2\x80\x99s first out loans with a premium, LOL. In many respects you have to admire the f*ckery of everyone here: first the company formed a new entity owned by Robertshaw (without voting interest, that went to a parent entity does this make it a \xe2\x80\x9csubsidiary?,\xe2\x80\x9d \xf0\x9f\xa4\x94); second, the lender plaintiffs loaned that new entity $228mm; third, the new entity dividended the $228mm to Robertshaw which used the proceeds to prepay the super- priority first- out loans in an amount that eliminated Invesco\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x9crequired lender\xe2\x80\x9d status. Now the \xe2\x80\x9crequired lenders,\xe2\x80\x9d the lender plaintiffs amended the credit agreement to allow Robertshaw to incur $228mm of new debt and repay the initial $228mm loan to the new entity. This structure was a workaround designed to avoid a covenant breach in the credit agreement which prohibited Robertshaw and its \xe2\x80\x9csubsidiaries\xe2\x80\x9d from incurring new debt. \xf0\x9f\x93\x8dInvesco was like, \xe2\x80\x9cwhat the holy f*ck\xe2\x80\x9d and sued the lender plaintiffs and One Rock in New York. Nevermind it got paid out at par plus on the first lien loans, the deal implicated other potential economics! \xf0\x9f\x93\x8dTwo months later Robertshaw filed for bankruptcy, making this yet another embarrassing example of a company that embarked on a comprehensive LME transaction (two actually) only to swirl around the dirtiest downtrodden bankruptcy bin imaginable. We\xe2\x80\x99re talking like straight out of Trainspotting , folks . The Robertshaw debtors along with One Rock and the lender plaintiffs filed an adversary proceeding against Invesco, to which Invesco shot over some counterclaims. Everyone wanted declaratory judgements! Everyone wanted to know what the bloody hell Judge Lopez was going to do in this high profile situation! Would he play ball with the debtor and the lender plaintiffs and establish his debtor- friendly bona fides? \xf0\x9f\x93\x8dMost people in the market seemed to suspect that he would, though Judge Lopez took offense at the mere suggestion that he might be a sure shot booster for the debtors. \xf0\x9f\x93\x8dJudge Lopez did conclude that the newly formed entity was a \xe2\x80\x9csubsidiary\xe2\x80\x9d for purposes of the credit agreement and therefore found that the loan to the newly formed entity breached the credit agreement. Invesco be like: Source: GIPHY \xf0\x9f\x93\x8dJudge Lopez be like\xe2\x80\xa6 Source: GIPHY... Subscribe to PETITION to unlock the rest. Become a paying subscriber of PETITION to get access to this post and other subscriber- only content. Upgrade to paid A subscription gets you: Subscribers'- only Wednesday and
